title: The Builders Behind Healthcare's Biggest Bet
content:
> Value-based care has been "the future" for twenty years. A new category of companies is finally making it the present — and they're worth understanding whether you're a provider, an investor, or a policymaker.

There's a peculiar thing about healthcare reform: the vision tends to arrive decades before the infrastructure. The idea that paying for outcomes rather than procedures would produce better care at lower cost has circulated in policy circles since at least the 1990s. It's been the animating logic behind ACOs, bundled payments, shared savings programs, and a dozen CMS experiments since. And yet, as of today, the majority of healthcare payments in the United States still reward volume.

## So what changed?
Not the vision — the plumbing.
A category of companies has quietly built the connective tissue that fee-for-service never needed and value-based care desperately does: risk-stratification engines, interoperability layers, [care management platforms](https://bluebrix.health/), contract modeling tools, and patient engagement infrastructure. Collectively, they're called value-based care enablers. And they are, without exaggeration, the reason the shift from volume to value is finally happening at scale rather than at the margins.

## What Is a VBC Enabler, Actually?
The term gets used loosely, so it's worth being precise. A value-based care enabler is any company — technology platform, services firm, or hybrid — whose core function is helping healthcare providers participate successfully in payment models that tie reimbursement to outcomes, quality, and cost efficiency.
That's a broad definition, and the market reflects it. The enabler ecosystem spans:

Population health platforms that aggregate patient data, identify care gaps, and surface risk flags before patients become high-cost cases
Care coordination tools that manage the flow of patients across settings, reduce redundancy, and keep care teams synchronized
Analytics and interoperability infrastructure that normalizes data from disparate EHRs and makes it actionable in real time
Patient engagement systems that extend the care relationship beyond the clinical encounter
Specialty-specific enablers that bring VBC models into cardiology, oncology, nephrology, and other domains where fee-for-service has long dominated
Practice enablement platforms that handle contracting, credentialing, financial modeling, and ACO participation on behalf of physician groups that couldn't manage it alone

What unites all of them is a shared premise: that the clinical and operational work required to succeed under value-based contracts is genuinely hard, and that most providers — particularly independent practices and smaller health systems — cannot build that capability internally at a reasonable cost or timeline.

## Why Now? Three Forces That Converged
The enabler market didn't materialize overnight. It's the product of three forces that have been building for years and are now reinforcing each other.
Policy pressure finally has teeth. CMS has set a concrete target: all Medicare beneficiaries in accountable care relationships by 2030. States are moving aggressively on Medicaid managed care models that reward care coordination and social determinant interventions. Commercial payers, facing sustained employer pressure to produce better outcomes at predictable costs, are proliferating shared-savings arrangements. The policy environment has shifted from nudging providers toward value-based care to expecting it.
The economics of fee-for-service are deteriorating. Health systems that once absorbed the inefficiencies of uncoordinated care — duplicated imaging, preventable readmissions, unmanaged chronic conditions cycling in and out of emergency departments — are finding those inefficiencies increasingly unaffordable. The margin compression of the past several years has converted philosophical resistance to value-based care into pragmatic openness. When the old model stops working financially, alternatives become more attractive.
The technology stack finally exists. FHIR has matured into a functional interoperability standard. Cloud infrastructure can ingest and normalize healthcare data at population scale. Machine learning models trained on claims and clinical data can generate risk predictions that are genuinely useful rather than academically interesting. The tooling that VBC enablers are built on didn't exist at the scale or cost point needed for broad deployment a decade ago. It does now.

## The Market in Numbers
The VBC enabler segment is projected to grow from roughly $3.5 billion in 2024 to over $10 billion by 2031 — a compound annual growth rate above 15% in an industry that rarely grows at double digits in any category.
That growth is being validated by capital markets. Infrastructure startups are raising substantial Series B and C rounds. Strategic acquirers are paying premium multiples for platforms with proven track records and strong client retention. The message from investors is consistent: this category is real, the demand is durable, and the window for market-share capture is open.
Provider sentiment reinforces the investment thesis. A significant majority of health system leaders now view VBC enabler partnerships as necessary infrastructure rather than optional supplements. And a growing share of organizations expect value-based contracts to represent a higher portion of their revenue in the near term than they do today — not because they prefer it philosophically, but because the financial incentive structure is pushing them there.

The Competitive Landscape: Three Distinct Archetypes
The enabler market has organized itself into three broad archetypes, each serving a different part of the value-based care puzzle.

## The Practice Enablers
Companies like Aledade, Privia Health, and agilon health work primarily with independent physician practices and physician groups, providing the infrastructure those organizations need to participate in ACOs and risk-based contracts without the overhead of building that infrastructure themselves.
What they offer is essentially a turnkey VBC operation: centralized contracting, performance analytics, care management resources, and the organizational scaffolding to aggregate dispersed practices into entities large enough to take meaningful risk. Aledade, as one prominent example, has generated hundreds of millions in shared savings for independent practices that would have had no viable path to ACO participation otherwise.
This archetype has captured a substantial share of the current enabler market, and for good reason — the independent practice segment is large, underserved by traditional health IT, and highly motivated by the prospect of earning performance bonuses without the capital investment of building an employed-physician enterprise.

## The Specialty Enablers
A distinct and fast-growing archetype has emerged around specific clinical domains where outcomes are measurable, costs are concentrated, and value-based models have a particularly compelling logic. Kidney care (Somatus, Strive Health), oncology (OneOncology), and advanced cardiology are the clearest examples.
What makes specialty enablement distinctive is that it requires genuine clinical depth, not just operational infrastructure. Managing a patient with chronic kidney disease through a capitated arrangement requires disease-specific care protocols, interdisciplinary team coordination, and deep familiarity with the payer landscape for that condition. General-purpose VBC platforms can provide some of this, but specialist enablers have built around it.
This segment remains under-penetrated relative to its potential. Most value-based payment innovation has occurred in primary care, and the extension of VBC logic into specialty care is still early. That means the growth runway is long.

## The Infrastructure Players
A third archetype focuses on the foundational technology layer — contract management, data normalization, AI-assisted clinical workflows, and provider enablement tooling — without necessarily taking on care management or practice operation responsibilities.
Companies like Navina (AI-assisted chart review), Pearl Health (risk stratification and performance analytics for primary care), and Arbital Health (care infrastructure for risk-bearing entities) represent this category. Many are earlier-stage, Series B or C, and are increasingly positioned as acquisition targets for larger platforms seeking to complete their capability stacks.
The infrastructure segment is where the most interesting technical innovation is happening. AI applications in particular — predictive risk modeling, clinical documentation automation, real-time gap-in-care identification — are moving quickly and differentiating platforms that have invested in them from those that haven't.

## The Persistent Barriers (And Why They Matter)
The enabler market is growing rapidly. It is not, however, frictionless. Several structural barriers continue to slow adoption and shape competitive dynamics.
Interoperability remains unfinished. Despite years of regulatory pressure and billions in EHR investment, most health systems still operate dozens of siloed data sources that don't exchange information cleanly. The FHIR mandate has helped, but implementation is uneven. For enablers whose value proposition depends on a unified patient view, the patchwork state of data infrastructure is a constant operational challenge — and a persistent differentiator between platforms that have solved it and those that haven't.
Financial risk aversion is rational. Transitioning from fee-for-service revenue, which is predictable even if modest, to risk-based arrangements, which can produce windfalls or losses depending on outcomes, is genuinely frightening for many provider organizations. Smaller practices in particular lack the actuarial expertise and cash reserves to absorb downside risk exposure. Enablers that address this through sophisticated financial modeling, stop-loss provisions, and shared risk pooling are solving a real problem, not just selling infrastructure.
Provider workflow friction is underestimated. The most sophisticated population health platform in the world fails if physicians don't use it. Adoption barriers in healthcare IT are notorious — time pressure during patient encounters, skepticism toward systems that slow workflow, and fatigue from prior implementations that didn't deliver — and VBC enablers are not immune. Platforms that embed into existing clinical workflows rather than requiring parallel systems have a structural advantage.
Regulatory complexity compounds everything. Value-based care programs involve navigating CMS program requirements, state insurance regulations, HIPAA compliance, quality measure reporting across multiple payers, and risk-bearing entity licensing that varies by state. This complexity is a cost driver for every participant and a barrier to entry that, paradoxically, protects established enablers while slowing adoption by new entrants and smaller provider organizations.

## What Success Actually Looks Like
The organizations that are thriving in value-based care — and the enablers that are winning with them — share a set of characteristics that go beyond technology selection.
Leadership alignment from the top. The providers succeeding in VBC have executives who have made a genuine organizational commitment to the model, not treating it as a revenue supplement to fee-for-service while hedging with traditional billing. That commitment translates into resource allocation, workflow redesign, and cultural change that no technology platform can substitute for.
A realistic timeline. The organizations that have struggled most in VBC are those that expected fast returns on what is fundamentally a multi-year operational transformation. Building the [care management](https://bluebrix.health/) capabilities, data infrastructure, and payer relationships that value-based success requires takes time. Enabler partnerships accelerate that timeline significantly — but they don't eliminate it.
A focus on the patient as a variable, not just a data point. Population health platforms that treat patients as risk scores to be managed rather than individuals with specific barriers to engagement consistently underperform their projections. The best enablers have invested heavily in patient communication, health literacy, and engagement infrastructure — because the 80% of health outcomes driven by factors outside clinical walls can only be influenced through genuine patient partnership.

## Strategic Implications
The rise of VBC enablers has different implications depending on where you sit.
For provider organizations, the practical question is no longer whether to engage with value-based care but how to build the capabilities to do it well. Internal development of a full VBC capability stack is feasible for large, well-resourced health systems with long timelines — but for most organizations, enabler partnerships offer faster go-live, shared risk, and access to capabilities that would take years to build. The gap assessment — what can we build, what must we buy, what can we partner for — is the right starting framework.
For investors, the market is at a particularly interesting inflection point. The segment is growing fast enough to attract attention but not yet crowded enough to have eliminated pricing power for well-differentiated platforms. Specialty expansion, AI capability development, and consolidation dynamics (both through acquisition and through organic scaling of leading platforms) are the themes worth tracking. Client retention rates and demonstrated outcome improvement are the metrics that matter most for evaluating platform durability.
For policymakers, the enabler ecosystem represents both an opportunity and a risk. The opportunity: these companies are doing much of the operational work of healthcare transformation that government programs can incentivize but cannot execute. The risk: market consolidation without adequate oversight could create dominant platforms that extract value from the system rather than adding it, or that fail to serve safety-net and rural providers who need enablement most. Governance frameworks for AI transparency, data sharing, and equitable access will shape whether this market's growth translates into population-level health improvement.

## What Comes Next
The enabler market five years from now will look different from today in several important ways.
AI will move from a differentiator to a baseline expectation. Predictive risk stratification, clinical documentation automation, and real-time care gap identification are still competitive advantages today. They will be table stakes within a few years. The platforms that are investing in AI capability now are building durable advantages; those that aren't are falling behind on a timeline they may not yet appreciate.
Specialty care will become the primary growth frontier. Primary care VBC enablement is relatively mature. The next significant growth opportunity is in oncology, cardiology, behavioral health, musculoskeletal care, and women's health — specialties with concentrated costs, measurable outcomes, and underbuilt VBC infrastructure. The specialty enablers entering those markets now are positioning for the expansion wave that follows.
Global adoption will emerge as a real market. The NHS in the United Kingdom, Middle Eastern sovereign health systems, and Asian markets are all piloting value-based models that require the same enabling infrastructure as the U.S. market. The companies that have built platform capabilities in the American context are well-positioned to extend internationally — and the market size implications are significant.
Patient experience will become a competitive differentiator in ways it currently isn't. The shift from patient as passive recipient to patient as active care partner is more aspiration than reality in most VBC programs today. The enablers that crack the engagement problem — not through better reminder systems, but through fundamentally reimagined communication models that meet patients where they are — will produce outcomes that outperform their competitors materially.

## The Honest Summary
Value-based care has been promised for a long time, and the promises have repeatedly outrun the delivery. That history creates warranted skepticism.
But the enabler ecosystem represents something genuinely new: companies whose business models succeed only when providers succeed under value-based contracts. That alignment of incentives is what makes this moment different from prior iterations of the same vision. The infrastructure is finally being built by people who have skin in the game — and built at a scale and technical sophistication that wasn't available before.
The transition from volume to value was always going to be slow, difficult, and contested. What VBC enablers have done is make it survivable — and, for the organizations that engage seriously with the model, potentially very profitable.
The market is real. The growth is durable. The window for building meaningful position is open but not indefinite.

_This analysis draws on publicly available market research, company filings, and industry reporting. It is intended for healthcare strategy, investment, and policy audiences and does not constitute financial or investment advice. Individual organizational outcomes in value-based care arrangements vary significantly._
